name: The underlying one-step method (GLM)
statement_text:
Let (A, U, B, V ) denote a consistent general linear method
such that u = e1 and such that

                                        ],                 1 v
                          U = [1       U          V =                     ,
                                                            0 V

where 1 ∈ σ(V ). Then there exists a unique solution to (535a) and (535b) for
which ξ1 = 1.
statement_latex:
\begin{theorem}[535A]
Let $(A, U, B, V)$ denote a consistent general linear method such that $u = e_1$ and such that
\[
U = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \widehat{U} \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & v^\top \\ 0 & \widehat{V} \end{bmatrix},
\]
where $1 \notin \sigma(\widehat{V})$. Then there exists a unique solution to \eqref{eq:535a} and \eqref{eq:535b} for which $\xi_1 = 1$.
\end{theorem}
proof_text:
Proof. By carrying out a further transformation if necessary, we may assume
without loss of generality that V is lower triangular. The conditions satisfied
by ξi (t) (i = 2, 3, . . . , r), ηi (t) (i = 1, 2, . . . , s) and θ(t) can now be written in
the form
                                    
                                    s                     
                                                          i−1
             (1 − Vi,i )ξi (t) =         bij (ηD)(t) +         Vi−1,j−1 ξj (t),
                                    j=1                   j=2

                                    
                                    s                               
                                                                    r
                        ηi (t) =          aij (ηD)(t) + 1(t) +            i,j−1 ξj (t),
                                                                          U
                                    j=1                             j=2

                                    
                                    s                               
                                                                    r
                          θ(t) =          b1j (ηD)(t) + 1(t) +            vj−1 ξj (t).
                                    j=1                             j=2

In each of these equations, the right-hand sides involve only trees with order
lower than r(t) or terms with order r(t) which have already been evaluated.
Hence, the result follows by induction on r(t).

variables.name: A
variables.definition: Part of the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: U
variables.definition: Part of the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: B
variables.definition: Part of the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: V
variables.definition: Part of the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: u
variables.definition: First column of U, equal to e1 (first natural basis vector in R^r)

variables.name: e1
variables.definition: First member of the natural basis for R^r

variables.name: ξ
variables.definition: A function (or vector) appearing in the method equations

variables.name: η
variables.definition: A function appearing in the method equations

variables.name: σ(Ṽ)
variables.definition: Spectrum (set of eigenvalues) of the matrix Ṽ

preamble: The theorem concerns a consistent general linear method (A, U, B, V) with u = e1, where U and V have specific block structures. The equations (535a) and (535b) define relationships involving η, ξ, and the method components, and the goal is to find a unique solution with ξ1 = 1.
